Transaction Cost Disclosure
ESOP Sale — Itemized Fee Estimate by Scenario
Company
{{companyName}}
Seller
{{sellerName}}
Reference
{{ref}}
Date
{{generatedDate}}
§ 01Purpose

This disclosure itemizes the estimated costs associated with completing an ESOP transaction. Three scenarios are provided to reflect the range of possible outcomes based on company size, complexity, and deal structure. All figures are estimates; final costs will be confirmed during the engagement process.

Note: Transaction costs are paid by the company as ordinary business expenses. They do not reduce seller proceeds and are not deducted from the seller's closing statement.
§ 02Fee Estimate by Scenario
Fee Category Low Realistic High
Forhemit Success Fee {{forhemitFeeLow}} {{forhemitFeeRealistic}} {{forhemitFeeHigh}}
ESOP Trustee Fee {{trusteeFeeLow}} {{trusteeFeeRealistic}} {{trusteeFeeHigh}}
ERISA Counsel {{erisaFeeLow}} {{erisaFeeRealistic}} {{erisaFeeHigh}}
Quality of Earnings (QofE) Firm {{qofeFeeLow}} {{qofeFeeRealistic}} {{qofeFeeHigh}}
Valuation / Appraisal Firm {{valuationFeeLow}} {{valuationFeeRealistic}} {{valuationFeeHigh}}
Other Advisors & Closing Costs {{otherFeeLow}} {{otherFeeRealistic}} {{otherFeeHigh}}
Total Estimated Transaction Costs {{totalLow}} {{totalRealistic}} {{totalHigh}}
§ 03Scenario Definitions
Straightforward deal, existing C-Corp, single class of stock, cooperative lender, experienced advisors.
Typical mid-market ESOP with moderate complexity, standard due diligence, and no material complications.
Complex deal structure (e.g., LLC conversion, multi-class stock, litigation history, or multi-state operations).
§ 04Comparison to Traditional M&A

For context, traditional third-party M&A transactions typically incur total advisory and closing costs of 8% to 12% of enterprise value. The ESOP transaction cost structure is generally lower because there is no sell-side M&A broker commission, and the trustee acts as the buyer's fiduciary rather than an intermediary seeking a commission.

Key distinction: In a third-party sale, the seller often pays a 4–6% broker commission plus legal and accounting fees. In an ESOP sale, the company pays all fees as business expenses, and there is no broker commission on the seller side.
§ 05IRC §1042 Tax Deferral Context

If the seller elects §1042 treatment and reinvests 100% of sale proceeds in Qualified Replacement Securities, the capital gains tax on the sale is deferred. This deferral can be worth significantly more than the total transaction costs, depending on the seller's basis and applicable tax rates. The seller's CPA should verify the applicability of §1042 based on the seller's specific circumstances.

Important: The §1042 election requires C-Corp status at the time of sale, a newly created ESOP (or one that has held less than 30% of outstanding stock), and reinvestment within a specified period. These requirements should be confirmed with the seller's tax advisor before engagement.
§ 06Contact

For questions about these estimates or to discuss your specific deal economics, please contact:
